concepts.title: Solving Homogeneous Differential Equation
concepts.conceptBody:
Solving Homogeneous Differential Equation

Q) Solve the following differential equation: 

(i) ${{y}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}}\frac{dy}{dx}=xy\frac{dy}{dx}$ (ii) $\left( {{\text{x}}^{3}}+3x{{y}^{2}} \right)dx+\left( {{y}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}y \right)dy=0$ 

(iii) ${{x}^{2}}dy+y\left( x+y \right)dx=0$, given that when $x=1,y=1$. 

Solution : 

(i) Given differential equation is

$\begin{align} & {{y}^{2}}+{{x}^{2}}\frac{dy}{dx}=xy\frac{dy}{dx} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \left( xy-{{x}^{2}} \right)\frac{dy}{dx}={{y}^{2}}\quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{{{y}^{2}}}{xy-{{x}^{2}}} \\ \end{align}$

Since degree of each term of numerator and denominator is same i..e, 2, therefore given equation is homogeneous.

Putting $y=vx\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}$

Given equation reduces to

$\begin{align} & \text{v}+\text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}{{\text{x}}^{2}}}{\text{xvx}-{{\text{x}}^{2}}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{v}-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}}{\text{v}-1}-\text{v}=\frac{{{\text{v}}^{2}}-{{\text{v}}^{2}}+\text{v}}{\text{v}-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=\frac{\text{v}}{\text{v}-1} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\text{v}-1}{\text{v}}\text{dv}=\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}} \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\begin{align} & \int{\frac{\text{v}-1}{\text{v}}}\text{dv}=\int{\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \int{1}-\frac{1}{\text{v}}\text{dv}=\log |\text{x}|+\text{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{v}-\log |\text{v}|=\log |\text{x}|+\text{c }\Rightarrow \frac{\text{y}}{\text{x}}-\log \left| \frac{\text{y}}{\text{x}} \right|=\log |\text{x}|+\text{c} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{\text{y}}{\text{x}}-\log |\text{y}|=\text{c} \\ \end{align}$

which is required solution.

(ii) Given differential equation is

$\left( {{x}^{3}}+3x{{y}^{2}} \right)dx+\left( {{y}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}y \right)dy=0\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{{{x}^{3}}+3x{{y}^{2}}}{{{y}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}y}$

Since degree of each term of numerator and denominator is same i..e, 3, therefore given equation is homogeneous equation.

Putting $y=vx\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}$

Given equation reduces to

$\begin{align} & v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=-\frac{{{x}^{3}}+3x{{v}^{2}}{{x}^{2}}}{{{v}^{3}}{{x}^{3}}+3{{x}^{2}}vx} \\ & \Rightarrow v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=-\frac{1+3{{v}^{2}}}{{{v}^{3}}+3v} \\ & \Rightarrow x\frac{dv}{dx}=-\frac{1+3{{v}^{2}}}{{{v}^{3}}+3v}=\frac{-1-3{{v}^{2}}-{{v}^{4}}-3{{v}^{2}}}{{{v}^{3}}+3v} \\ & \Rightarrow x\frac{dv}{dx}=-\frac{1+6{{v}^{2}}+{{v}^{4}}}{{{v}^{3}}+3v}\Rightarrow \frac{{{v}^{3}}+3v}{1+6{{v}^{2}}+{{v}^{4}}}dv=-\frac{dx}{x} \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\begin{align} & \int{\frac{{{v}^{3}}+3v}{1+6{{v}^{2}}+{{v}^{4}}}}dx=-\int{\frac{dx}{x}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}\int{\frac{dt}{t}}=-\int{\frac{dx}{x}} \\ \end{align}$

where $t=1+6{{v}^{2}}\Rightarrow dt=\left( 12v+4{{v}^{3}} \right)dv\Rightarrow dt=4\left( 3v+{{v}^{3}} \right)dv$

\(\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{4}\log |t|=-\log |x|+\log c\Rightarrow \frac{1}{4}\log |t|=\log \frac{c}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \log |t|=\log {{\left( \frac{c}{x} \right)}^{4}}\Rightarrow t=\frac{{{c}^{4}}}{{{x}^{4}}} \\ & \Rightarrow 1+6{{v}^{2}}+{{v}^{4}}=\frac{{{c}^{4}}}{{{x}^{4}}}\Rightarrow 1+\frac{6{{y}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}}+\frac{{{y}^{4}}}{{{x}^{4}}}=\frac{{{c}^{4}}}{{{x}^{4}}} \\ & \Rightarrow {{x}^{4}}+6{{x}^{2}}{{y}^{2}}+{{y}^{4}}={{c}^{4}}\text{ } \\ \end{align}\)

which is the required solution.

(iii) Given differential equation is

${{x}^{2}}dy+y\left( x+y \right)dx=0\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{yx+{{y}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}}$

Putting $y=vx\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}$

Given equation reduces to

$\begin{align} & v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=-\frac{v{{x}^{2}}+{{v}^{2}}{{x}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}}\Rightarrow v+x\frac{dv}{dx}=-v-{{v}^{2}} \\ & \Rightarrow x\frac{dv}{dx}=-2v-{{v}^{2}}=-\left( 2v+{{v}^{2}} \right) \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{dv}{2v+{{v}^{2}}}=-\frac{dx}{x} \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\int{\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{v}(2+\text{v})}}=-\int{\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}}}\quad \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}\int{\left[ \frac{1}{\text{v}}-\frac{1}{2+\text{v}} \right]}\text{dv}=-\int{\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}}}$

$\Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\int{\frac{1}{v}}dv-\frac{1}{2}\int{\frac{1}{2+v}}dv=-\int{\frac{dx}{x}}$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\log |v|-\frac{1}{2}\log |2+v|=-\log |x|+\log c \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\log \left| \frac{v}{2+v} \right|=\log \frac{c}{x} \\ & \Rightarrow \log \left| \frac{v}{2+v} \right|=\log \frac{{{c}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{v}{2+v}=\frac{{{c}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{\frac{y}{x}}{2+\frac{y}{x}}=\frac{{{c}^{2}}}{{{x}^{2}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{{{x}^{2}}y}{2x+y}={{c}^{2}} \\ \end{align}$

When $\text{x}=1,\text{y}=1\Rightarrow \frac{1}{3}={{\text{c}}^{2}}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ Rquired solution is $\frac{{{\text{x}}^{2}}\text{y}}{2\text{x}+\text{y}}=\frac{1}{3}\Rightarrow 3{{\text{x}}^{2}}\text{y}=2\text{x}+\text{y}$

 

Q) Solve the following differential equation : 

(a) $\left( x\text{cos}\frac{y}{x} \right)\left( ydx+xdy \right)=\left( y\text{sin}\frac{y}{x} \right)\left( xdy-ydx \right)$ (b) $\left( 1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}/\text{y}}} \right)\text{dx}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}/\text{y}}}\left( 1-\frac{\text{x}}{\text{y}} \right)d\text{y}=0$ 

(c) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }fy\left( xy+{{e}^{xy}} \right)dx+x\left( xy-{{e}^{xy}} \right)dy=0$. 

Solution : 

(a) Given differential equation is

$\begin{align} & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\left( x\cos \frac{y}{x} \right)(ydx+xdy)=\left( y\sin \frac{y}{x} \right)(xdy-ydx) \\ & \Rightarrow \quad xy\cos \frac{y}{x}dx+{{x}^{2}}\cos \frac{y}{x}dy=xy\sin \frac{y}{x}dy-{{y}^{2}}\sin \frac{y}{x}dx \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \left( xy\cos \frac{y}{x}+{{y}^{2}}\sin \frac{y}{x} \right)dx+\left( {{x}^{2}}\cos \frac{y}{x}-xy\sin \frac{y}{x} \right)dy=0\quad \\ & \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{dy}{dx}=-\frac{xy\cos \frac{y}{x}+{{y}^{2}}\sin \frac{y}{x}}{{{x}^{2}}\cos \frac{y}{x}-xy\sin \frac{y}{x}}=\frac{\frac{y}{x}\cos \frac{y}{x}+{{\left( \frac{y}{x} \right)}^{2}}\sin \frac{y}{x}}{\cos \frac{y}{x}-\frac{y}{x}\sin \frac{y}{x}} \\ \end{align}$

Since $\frac{dy}{dx}$ is a function of $\frac{y}{x}$, therefore, given equation is homogeneous.

Putting $y=vx\,\,\,\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\Rightarrow \frac{dy}{dx}=v+x\frac{dv}{dx}$

Given equation reduces to

$\begin{align} & \text{v}+\text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=-\frac{\text{v}\cos \text{v}+{{\text{v}}^{2}}\sin \text{v}}{\cos \text{v}-\text{v}\sin \text{v}} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=-\frac{\text{v}\cos \text{v}+{{\text{v}}^{2}}\sin \text{v}}{\cos \text{v}-\text{v}\sin \text{v}}-\text{v}=\frac{-\text{v}\cos \text{v}-{{\text{v}}^{2}}\sin \text{v}-\text{v}\cos \text{v}+{{\text{v}}^{2}}\sin \text{v}}{\cos \text{v}-\text{v}\sin \text{v}} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{x}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dx}}=-\frac{2\text{v}\cos \text{v}}{\cos \text{v}-\text{v}\sin \text{v}}\Rightarrow \frac{\cos \text{v}-\text{v}\sin \text{v}}{\text{v}\cos \text{v}}\text{dv}=-2\frac{\text{dx}}{\text{x}} \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\int ~\frac{\text{cos}v-v\text{sin}v}{v\text{cos}v}dv=-2\int ~\frac{dx}{x}$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \log |v\cos v|=-2\log x+\log c\quad \\ & \Rightarrow \log |v\cos v|=\log \frac{c}{{{x}^{2}}} \\ & \Rightarrow v\cos v=\frac{c}{{{x}^{2}}}\quad \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{y}{x}\cos \frac{y}{x}=\frac{c}{{{x}^{2}}} \\ & \Rightarrow xy\cos \frac{y}{x}=c \\ \end{align}$

which is the required solution.

(b) Given differential equation is

$\begin{align} & \left( 1+{{e}^{x/y}} \right)dx+{{e}^{x/y}}\left( 1-\frac{x}{y} \right)dy=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{-1+{{e}^{x/y}}}{{{e}^{x/y}}\left( 1-\frac{x}{y} \right)}\quad \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{dx}{dy}=\frac{-{{e}^{x/y}}\left( 1-\frac{x}{y} \right)}{1+{{e}^{x/y}}} \\ \end{align}$

Putting \(\frac{x}{y}=v\Rightarrow x=vy\Rightarrow \frac{dx}{dy}=v+y\frac{dv}{dy}\)

Given equation reduces to

$\begin{align} & \text{v}+\text{y}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dy}}=\frac{-{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}(1-\text{v})}{1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}\Rightarrow \text{y}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dy}}=\frac{-{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}(1-\text{v})}{1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}-\text{v} \\ & \Rightarrow \text{y}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dy}}=\frac{-{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}+\text{v}{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}-\text{v}-\text{v}{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}{1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}} \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \text{ y}\frac{\text{dv}}{\text{dy}}=-\frac{{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}+\text{v}}{1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}\quad \,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \frac{1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}{\text{v}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}\text{dv}=-\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{y}} \\ \end{align}$

Integrating both sides, we get

$\int ~\frac{1+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}{\text{v}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}}\text{dv}=-\int ~\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{y}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \text{log}\left| \text{v}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}} \right|=-\text{log}\left| \text{y} \right|+\text{logc}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ log}\left| \text{v}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}} \right|=\text{log}\frac{\text{c}}{\text{y}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \text{v}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{v}}}=\frac{\text{c}}{\text{y}}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{x}}{\text{y}}+{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}/\text{y}}}=\frac{\text{c}}{\text{y}}\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\,\Rightarrow \text{x}+\text{y}{{\text{e}}^{\text{x}/\text{y}}}=\text{c}$

which is the required solution.

  1. Let $xy=u\Rightarrow xdy+ydx=d$

Now given equation can be written as $x{{y}^{2}}dx+{{x}^{2}}ydy+y\cdot {{e}^{xy}}dx-x\cdot {{e}^{xy}}dx-x{{e}^{xy}}dy=0$

$\begin{align} & \Rightarrow \quad xy(ydx+xdy)+{{e}^{xy}}(ydx-xdy)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad xy(ydx+xdy)+{{e}^{xy}}(ydx+xdy-2xdy)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad udu+{{e}^{u}}\left( du-2\cdot \frac{u}{y}dy \right)=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \,\,\,\,\,udu+{{e}^{u}}du-\frac{2u\cdot {{e}^{u}}}{y}\cdot dy=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\int{\left( \frac{u+{{e}^{u}}}{u\cdot {{e}^{u}}} \right)}du-\int{\frac{dy}{y}}=0 \\ & \Rightarrow \quad \frac{1}{2}\left( \log |u|+\log \left| {{e}^{u}} \right| \right)=\log |y|-c \\ & \Rightarrow \frac{1}{2}(\log |xy|+xy)=\log |y|+c \\ \end{align}$

concepts.title: Non-Homogeneous Differential Equations reducible to Homogeneous form
concepts.conceptBody:
Non-homogeneous differential equations reducible to homogeneous form 

Equation of the form

$\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{{{a}_{1}}x+{{b}_{1}}y+{{c}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}}$

Can be reduced to a homogeneous form by putting $\text{x}=\text{X}+\alpha $ and $\text{y}=\text{Y}+\beta $, where $\alpha $ and $\beta $ are constants which will be determined so that the differential equation becomes homogeneous.

When $\text{x}=\text{X}+\alpha ,\text{y}=\text{Y}+\beta ,\text{dx}=\text{dX},\text{dy}=\text{dY}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dy}{dx}\ne \frac{dY}{dX}$

Now equation (1) becomes

$\frac{dY}{dX}=\frac{{{a}_{1}}\left( X+\alpha \right)+{{b}_{1}}\left( Y+\beta \right)+{{c}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}\left( X+\alpha \right)+{{b}_{2}}\left( Y+\beta \right)+{{c}_{2}}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dY}{dX}=\frac{{{a}_{1}}X+{{b}_{1}}Y+{{a}_{1}}\alpha +{{b}_{1}}\beta +{{c}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}X+{{b}_{2}}Y+{{a}_{2}}\alpha +{{b}_{2}}\beta +{{c}_{2}}}$

We choose $\alpha $ and $\beta $ such that

${{\text{a}}_{1}}\alpha +{{\text{b}}_{1}}\beta +{{\text{c}}_{1}}=0$

and ${{\text{a}}_{2}}\alpha +{{\text{b}}_{2}}\beta +{{\text{c}}_{2}}=0$

so that $\frac{\alpha }{{{b}_{1}}{{c}_{2}}-{{b}_{2}}{{c}_{1}}}=\frac{\beta }{{{c}_{1}}{{a}_{2}}-{{c}_{2}}{{a}_{1}}}=\frac{1}{{{a}_{1}}{{b}_{2}}-{{a}_{2}}{{b}_{1}}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\alpha =\frac{{{\text{b}}_{1}}{{\text{c}}_{2}}-{{\text{b}}_{2}}{{\text{c}}_{1}}}{{{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}}-{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{1}}},\beta =\frac{{{\text{c}}_{1}}{{\text{a}}_{2}}-{{\text{c}}_{2}}{{\text{a}}_{1}}}{{{\text{a}}_{1}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{2}}-{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }{{\text{b}}_{1}}}$

These values of $\alpha $ and $\beta $ are possible only when $\frac{{{a}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}}\ne \frac{{{b}_{1}}}{{{b}_{2}}}$.

For these values of $\alpha $ and $\beta $ equation (2) becomes

$\frac{dY}{dX}=\frac{{{a}_{1}}X+{{b}_{1}}Y}{{{a}_{2}}X+{{b}_{2}}Y}$ ...(3)

Equation (3) is homogeneous and can be solved by putting $\text{Y}=\text{vX}$.

Finally replacing $\text{X}$ by $\text{x}-\alpha $ and $\text{Y}$ by $\text{y}-\beta $ in the solution thus obtained we will get the required solution.

Special Case. 

If $\frac{{{a}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}}=\frac{{{b}_{1}}}{{{b}_{2}}}=k(say),$ then method given above fails. In this case equation (1) becomes

$\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{{{a}_{2}}kx+{{b}_{2}}ky+{{c}_{1}}}{{{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y+{{c}_{2}}}$

$=\frac{\text{k}\left( {{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y} \right)+{{\text{c}}_{1}}}{{{\text{a}}_{2}}\text{x}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\text{y}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}}$ ...(4)

Putting $z={{a}_{2}}x+{{b}_{2}}y$, we get

$\begin{align} & \,\,\,\,\,\,\frac{\text{dz}}{\text{dx}}={{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \\ & \therefore \,\,\,\,\left( \frac{\text{dz}}{\text{dx}}-{{\text{a}}_{2}} \right)={{\text{b}}_{2}}\frac{\text{dy}}{\text{dx}} \\ \end{align}$

$={{\text{b}}_{2}}\left( \frac{\text{kz}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}}{\text{z}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}} \right)$ [from (4)]

or $\frac{\text{dz}}{\text{dx}}={{\text{a}}_{2}}+{{\text{b}}_{2}}\left( \frac{\text{kz}+{{\text{c}}_{1}}}{\text{z}+{{\text{c}}_{2}}} \right)$

Now the variables can be separated.

 

Q) Solve the differential equation $\left( x+y+1 \right)dx-\left( 2x+2y+1 \right)dy=0$ 

Solution : 

Given differential equation can be written as

$\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x+y+1}{2x+2y+1}$ ...(1)

Put $z=2x+2y$, then $\frac{dz}{dx}=2+2\frac{dy}{dx}$ or $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{1}{2}\frac{dz}{dx}-1$

$\frac{1}{2}\frac{\text{dz}}{\text{dx}}-1=\frac{\frac{\text{z}}{2}+1}{\text{z}+1}=\frac{\text{z}+2}{2\text{z}+2}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{dz}}{\text{dx}}=2\left( \frac{\text{z}+2}{2\text{z}+2}+1 \right)=\frac{2\left( 3\text{z}+4 \right)}{2\text{z}+2}=\frac{3\text{x}+4}{\text{z}+1}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{z+1}{3z+4}dz=dx$ or $\int \frac{z+1}{3z+4}dz=\int dx$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{3}\int \frac{3z+3}{3z+4}dz=\int dx$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{3}\int \frac{3z+4-1}{3z+4}\text{dz}=\int \text{dz}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{3}\int \left( 1-\frac{1}{3z+4} \right)dz=\int dx$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{3}\text{z}-\frac{1}{9}\text{log}\left( 3\text{z}+4 \right)=\text{x}+\text{k}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }3\text{z}-\text{log}\left( 3\text{z}+4 \right)=9\text{x}+9\text{k}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }6x+6y-\text{log}\left[ 6x+6y+4 \right]=9x+9k$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ y}=\frac{1}{2}\text{x}+\frac{1}{6}\text{log}\left[ 6\left( \text{x}+\text{y} \right)+4 \right]+\text{c}$, where $\text{c}=\frac{3}{2}\text{k}$

 

Q) Find the solution of differential equation $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x+2y-3}{2x+y-3}$ 

Solution : 

Given equation is $\frac{dy}{dx}=\frac{x+2y-3}{2x+y-3}$ is ...(i)

Putting $\text{x}=\text{X}+\text{h},\text{y}=\text{Y}+\text{k}$ in equation (i)

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\text{dx}=\text{dX}\And \text{dy}=\text{dY}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ from (i) $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{\text{dY}}{\text{dX}}=\frac{\text{X}+\text{h}+2\left( \text{Y}+\text{k} \right)-3}{2\left( \text{X}+\text{h} \right)+\text{Y}+\text{k}-3}$

$=\frac{\left( \text{X}+2\text{Y} \right)+\left( \text{h}+2\text{k}-3 \right)}{\left( 2\text{X}+\text{Y} \right)+\left( 2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}+\text{k}-3 \right)}$ ...(ii)

Now choosing $h\And \text{k}$ such that

$\text{h}+2\text{k}-3=0$

and $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }2\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ h}+\text{k}-3=0$

Solving by cross multiplication $\frac{\text{h}}{-6+3}=\frac{\text{k}}{-6+3}=\frac{1}{1-4}$

$\Rightarrow \text{h}=1,\text{k}=1$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ equation (ii) becomes $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{dY}{dX}=\frac{X+2Y}{2X+Y}$ ...(iii)

Putting $Y=vX$, then $\frac{dY}{dX}=v+X\frac{dv}{dX}$

$\therefore \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }$ from (iv), $v+X\frac{dv}{dX}=\frac{1+2v}{2+v}$,

Separating the variables, we get

$X\frac{dv}{dX}=\frac{1-{{v}^{2}}}{2+v}\Rightarrow \frac{2+v}{1-{{v}^{2}}}dv=\frac{dX}{X}$

Integrating both sides, $\int \frac{2+v}{1-{{v}^{2}}}dv=\int \frac{dX}{X}+{{c}_{1}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\int \frac{2+v}{\left( 1+v \right)\left( 1-v \right)}dv=\text{log}X+{{c}_{1}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1}{2}\text{log}\left( 1+v \right)-\frac{3}{2}\text{log}\left( 1-\text{v} \right)=\text{logX}+\text{c}$

$\Rightarrow \text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\frac{1+\text{v}}{{{(1-\text{v})}^{3}}}=\text{c}{{\text{X}}^{2}}$

or $\frac{1+\frac{\text{Y}}{\text{X}}}{{{\left( 1-\frac{\text{Y}}{\text{X}} \right)}^{3}}}=\text{c}{{\text{X}}^{2}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ X}+\text{Y}=\text{c}{{(\text{X}-\text{Y})}^{3}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\text{x}-1+\text{y}-1=\text{c}{{[\left( \text{x}-1 \right)-\left( \text{y}-1 \right)]}^{3}}$

or $\text{ }\!\!~\!\!\text{ }\text{x}+\text{y}-2=\text{c}{{(\text{x}-\text{y})}^{3}}$

examples.title: The solution of the differential equation $x^2 \frac{d y}{d x}=x^2+x y+y^2$ is
Solution of differential equation $2 x y \frac{d y}{d x}=x^2+3 y^2$ is
The solution of the differential equation $\left(x^2+y^2\right) d x=2 x y d y$ is
The solution of the differential equation $\left(3 x y+y^2\right) d x+\left(x^2+x y\right) d y=0$ is
The solution of the differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x y}{x^2+y^2}$ is
Solution of differential equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{y-x}{y+x}$ is
The solution of the equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x+y}{x-y}$ is
The solution of the equation $\frac{d y}{d x}=\frac{x}{2 y-x}$ is
The general solution of the differential equation $(2 x-y+1) d x+(2 y-x+1) d y=0$ is
